title: Invalid Data Received
system: Invalid Data Received
possible_causes: Invalid Data Received fault.
Invalid, missing, implausible, incompatible, or incorrectly formatted network data received from the named control module or message source.
Module software, coding/configuration, calibration level, network integrity, power/ground, and related module DTC influence are vehicle-specific and must be confirmed in official service information.
check_order: Save all-module DTCs, freeze-frame data, network topology, module part numbers, calibration IDs, coding/configuration data, battery voltage, and related communication or power supply DTCs.

Confirm vehicle-specific network architecture, module identity, signal ownership, gateway routing, software compatibility, coding/configuration, and diagnostic prerequisites in the manufacturer service manual or OEM service system.
Inspect module power/ground, network wiring, termination, connector condition, installed module part numbers, calibration levels, and configuration/coding consistency before replacing modules.

Separate invalid source data, wrong module/configuration, incomplete programming, gateway/network routing, power supply, sensor input, and receiving-module fault causes using the manufacturer procedure.
common_mistakes: Replacing the receiving module before checking the source module, network data validity, coding, and calibration.
Assuming signal names, message IDs, wiring pins, or normal values are common across vehicles.
Clearing or programming modules without saving the pre-repair scan, configuration data, and recovery information.
safety_notes: Use strong caution around brake, steering, SRS/restraints, high-voltage, ADAS, fuel, exhaust aftertreatment, and programming/coding related modules; invalid network data can affect safety-critical systems, so follow OEM disable, support, initialization, and recovery procedures.
source: SAE J2012 / SAE J2012DA generic DTC definitions and Autel official DTC table based diagnostic guidance
